lens.included_activities: US convention, conference, and trade-show organizers in the lower middle market that repeatedly plan, promote, manage, or own business events for external customers, exhibitors, sponsors, or attendees.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house event departments, venue-only operators, social-event planners outside NAICS 561920, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without recurring or repeat outsourced-service economics.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include associations, corporations, exhibitors, sponsors, and attendees. Revenue can combine management or production fees with exhibit-space, sponsorship, registration, ancillary-event, media, and digital-service revenue; contracts and advance bookings commonly precede the event.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe the occupation rather than wage-weighted tasks inside lens firms, while PCMA measures reported AI uses among a small, association-heavy planner sample. I map research, copywriting, agenda development, registration, bids, schedules, attendee communications, invoice review, and reporting to exposed work, then reduce for sales, negotiation, creative judgment, physical inspection, live coordination, and exception handling. The occupation proxy may overstate exposure if owner-sales and on-site labor carry more wages in LMM organizers.
a.rationale: A majority of planning-office work is information handling and coordination that current AI can assist or partially substitute. PCMA reports use concentrated in research, marketing copy, and agenda creation, while BLS duties reveal additional document-heavy workflows; the remaining live-production and relationship work limits full task exposure.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study or payroll-linked task survey of 10-99 employee US event-organizer firms, split between owned-event and fee-for-service models, would materially change this range.
caveats: Occupation task lists do not supply task hours or wage weights for NAICS 561920.
PCMA's 2025 survey had 80 respondents and was dominated by associations rather than independent organizers.

rho.bridge: PCMA planner surveys measure current adoption and use cases, not the five-year implemented share of exposed wage work in LMM organizer firms. I translate 65% reported adoption and broad expected importance into moderate implementation, then discount because advanced operational uses remain uncommon, governance is incomplete, and a separate survey found many planners saw no immediate need. This may overstate implementation if adoption is mostly occasional drafting, or understate it as event software embeds AI by default.
rho.rationale: Adoption is already material, but use is concentrated in low-risk research and content. Only a minority of surveyed planners expected lower staffing, and policy, data integration, quality control, client approval, and change-management constraints should prevent implementation of all exposed opportunity within five years.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal benchmark from PCMA, IAEE, or an event-technology vendor reporting production usage, hours eliminated, error rates, and headcount avoided by workflow in independent organizer firms would materially move rho.
caveats: Adoption is not equivalent to production deployment or labor substitution.
Survey respondents include internal planners and suppliers outside the exact lens.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently external-service oriented, and recurring annual shows, conferences, and repeat corporate or association programs should make many LMM firms eligible. The discount removes one-off producers, captive or venue-like operations, owners of fragile single events, and firms whose EBITDA does not normalize into the stated band.
e.change_evidence: A Census microdata tabulation or commercial-business-list sample for NAICS 561920 showing revenue model, client repeat rate, event portfolio, independence, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source directly measures the share of firms satisfying both repeat-service and normalized-EBITDA criteria.
Owned-event businesses and third-party management firms can have materially different transferability.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among eligible event organizers. Sector reporting of 73 event transactions in 2025 and Emerald's acquisition strategy supports active buyer interest but covers a broader, global events market and lacks a firm denominator. I anchor near the broad-owner intention rate and widen for completion risk and sector consolidation; intentions tend to overstate completed transfers, while reported transactions undercount private deals.
s5.rationale: Broad succession intentions imply a meaningful but minority five-year transfer pool, and the events market has strategic and private-equity buyers acquiring event portfolios and organizer assets. Failed processes, internal transfers, subscale firms, and owner-dependent client relationships reduce qualifying completion probability.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, or Axial transaction counts matched to a verified US NAICS 561920 LMM firm denominator, plus seller-age and close-rate data, would materially change s5.
caveats: The owner-intention survey is cross-industry and intentions are not completed control transactions.
The reported event deal count is global and includes adjacent event, media, and technology businesses.

q.rationale: Owned-event economics and fixed management fees allow organizers to retain part of AI-driven labor savings between renewals, while diversified exhibit-space, sponsorship, registration, and media revenue is not mechanically tied to labor cost. Competitive rebids, transparent cost-plus arrangements, client sharing, and renewal repricing should return a substantial portion of benefits to customers over five years.
q.change_evidence: A contract sample separating fixed-fee, cost-plus, revenue-share, and owned-event revenue, with renewal repricing and gross-margin bridges after automation, would materially change q.
caveats: No direct evidence measures benefit pass-through or renewal repricing in LMM organizer contracts.
Retention likely varies sharply between owned-event portfolios and agency-style event management.

d5.bridge: IAEE's CEIR forecast measures B2B exhibition activity over a near-term industry horizon, PCMA surveys planner expectations rather than realized constant-price quantities, and BLS projects employment for the broader planner occupation. I convert moderate growth and generally stable-to-rising in-person attendance into a modest five-year quantity increase, net of price and quality. Forecast optimism could overstate demand if trade, travel, funding, or recession shocks persist; occupation growth may include adjacent event types.
d5.rationale: Current evidence supports durable rather than explosive demand: IAEE forecast 2.1% B2B exhibition growth for 2026, almost half of PCMA respondents expected increased in-person attendance, and BLS projects planner employment growth. Budget cuts, travel expense, trade policy, and geopolitical uncertainty justify a downside near slight contraction.
d5.change_evidence: CEIR's annual Total Index components through 2028, adjusted to constant-price attendance, exhibitors, and net square feet for US independent organizers, would materially change d5.
caveats: Forecasts are not observations of year-five constant-price, constant-quality demand.
PCMA's small association-heavy sample is not representative of all NAICS 561920 firms.

o.bridge: BLS task descriptions and PCMA attendance evidence show work and demand that remain physical and relationship-intensive, but neither directly measures the share of year-five service quantity requiring a third-party accountable organizer. I retain tasks involving contracts, vendor coordination, venue inspection, travel, safety, live execution, sales, and exception ownership, while subtracting small/simple events that customers can self-serve with software. This may overstate operator need where integrated platforms enable internal teams, or understate it for large regulated events.
o.rationale: Software can self-serve registration, communications, content, and simple meeting logistics, but sizable conventions and trade shows still require an accountable party to assemble venues, suppliers, exhibitors, sponsors, programming, and live operations. The continued value attributed to in-person networking and high fully in-person share supports persistent operator demand.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and event-platform telemetry showing the share of comparable events moved from full-service organizers to client self-service, segmented by attendee count and complexity, would materially change o.
caveats: The operator-required share is inferred from workflows and attendance, not directly observed.
Small meetings are more self-serviceable than large trade shows, creating substantial within-code variation.

narrative.executive_view: Convention and trade-show organizers combine a sizable information-work automation surface with physical, relationship-heavy delivery that should preserve the need for an accountable operator. The opportunity is strongest in repeat programs or owned-event portfolios with standardized workflows and diversified revenue, but transfer volume and benefit retention remain estimated rather than directly measured.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can compress research, proposal and RFP drafting, marketing copy, agenda development, attendee communications, registration support, scheduling, vendor comparison, invoice review, and post-event reporting. Human work remains central in commercial relationships, creative judgment, negotiation, venue and supplier management, safety, live exception handling, and final accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed fees and owned-event revenue can preserve labor savings, especially between contract renewals, and revenue streams such as exhibit space, sponsorship, and registrations are not direct labor pass-throughs. Capture erodes where work is cost-plus, clients demand shared savings, competitors reprice, or automation makes basic planning easier to self-source.
narrative.firm_availability: The code is largely made up of external-service businesses, but not every firm has recurring clients, diversified event rights, transferable relationships, or LMM earnings. Broad owner succession intentions and active events M&A suggest a real acquisition pool, although no US NAICS-specific close-rate denominator supports a precise estimate.
narrative.demand_durability: Business events continue to support networking, product discovery, sponsorship, education, and relationship formation that digital channels do not fully replace. Near-term exhibition forecasts and planner expectations are positive overall, while funding cuts, travel costs, trade policy, geopolitics, and venue inflation create meaningful downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main risks are event cyclicality, cancellations, customer and flagship-show concentration, dependence on venue and travel ecosystems, owner-held relationships, working-capital timing, and sharp variation between owned-event and fee-for-service models. The largest evidence gaps are firm-level task weights, realized AI labor savings, contract repricing, and NAICS-specific completed control transfers.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeatable planning-office workflows can be automated while valuable in-person events still need coordinated, accountable execution.
narrative.principal_weakness: Benefits and transferability vary sharply by event ownership and contract model, with demand exposed to travel, budgets, and disruption shocks.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 561920 Convention and Trade Show Organizers
sources.supports: Industry scope: organizing, promoting, and/or managing business and trade shows, conventions, conferences, and meetings.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Meeting, Convention, and Event Planners
sources.supports: Planner duties, physical and coordination work, 2024 employment, and 2024-2034 employment outlook.

sources.title: O*NET: Meeting, Convention, and Event Planners
sources.supports: Detailed task inventory spanning customer consultation, event coordination, records, billing, suppliers, and logistics.

sources.title: Convene's 32nd Meetings Market Survey Results
sources.supports: 2025 planner survey on AI adoption and use cases, staffing expectations, in-person attendance, digital formats, budgets, and business-model changes.

sources.title: Survey: How Event Planners Are Using AI for Meetings in 2025
sources.supports: Current planner AI applications, including relatively limited reported use in event planning and logistics.

sources.title: Study Identifies Missed Opportunities to Use AI in Event Planning
sources.supports: 2025 survey evidence on event-professional AI adoption constraints and perceived lack of immediate need.

sources.title: Emerald Holding, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Organizer business model, revenue streams, advance contracting, recurring participation, pricing, acquisition strategy, and customer value proposition.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Cross-industry five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among US employer-business owners.

sources.title: The Business of Acquisitions: Competing with Private Equity to Buy Shows
sources.supports: Reported 2025 events transaction count and examples of acquisition activity in event organizers and event assets.

sources.title: Census NAPCS Product List for Administrative and Support Services, NAICS 5619
sources.supports: Convention and trade-show organization services sold for a fee or commission, including planning, organizing, marketing, and managing non-social events.

sources.title: IAEE Releases 2026 CEIR Index Report
sources.supports: B2B exhibition performance and forecast, including 2026 growth expectations, participation indicators, forward bookings, and macro risks.
